title: AI, Vim, And the illusion of flow
excerpt: I’ve been using AI in my job a lot more lately — and it’s becoming an explicit expectation across the industry. Write more code, deliver more features, ship faster. You know what this makes me think about? Vim.
content_html:
I’ve been using AI in my job a lot more lately — and it’s becoming an explicit expectation across the industry. Write more code, deliver more features, ship faster. You know what this makes me think about? Vim.

I’ll explain myself, don’t worry.

I like Vim. Enough to write a book about the editor, and enough to use Vim to write this article. I’m sure you’ve encountered colleagues who swear by their Vim or Emacs setups, or you might be one yourself.

Here’s the thing most people get wrong about Vim: it isn’t about speed. It doesn’t necessarily make you faster (although it can), but what it does is keep you in the flow. It makes text editing easier — it’s nice not having to hunt down the mouse or hold an arrow key for exactly three and a half seconds. You can just delete a sentence. Or replace text inside the parentheses, or maybe swap parentheses for quotes. You’re editing without interruption, and it gives your brain space to focus on the task at hand.

AI tools look this way on the surface. They promise the same thing Vim delivers: less friction, more flow, your brain freed up to think about the hard stuff. And sometimes they actually deliver on that promise! I’ve had sessions where an AI assistant helped me skip past the tedious scaffolding and jump straight into the interesting architectural problem. There’s lots of good here.

Well, I think the difference between AI and Vim explains a lot of the discomfort engineers are feeling right now.

The depth problem

When I use Vim, the output is mine. Every keystroke, every motion, every edit — it’s a direct translation of my intent. Vim is a transparent tool: it does exactly what I tell it to do, nothing more. The skill floor and ceiling are high, but the relationship is honest. I learn a new motion, I understand what it does, and I can predict its behavior forever. There’s no hallucination. ci) will always change text inside parentheses. It won’t sometimes change the whole paragraph because it misunderstood the context.

AI tools have a different relationship with their operator. The output looks like yours, reads like yours, and certainly looks more polished than what you would produce on a first pass. But it isn’t a direct translation of your intent. Sometimes it’s a fine approximation. Sometimes it’s subtly wrong in ways you won’t catch until a hidden bug hits production.

This is what I’d call the depth problem. When I use Vim, nobody can tell from reading my code whether I wrote it in Vim, VS Code, or Notepad. The tool is invisible in the artifact. And that’s fine, great even - because the quality of the output still depends entirely on me. My understanding of the problem, my experience with the codebase, my judgment about edge cases, my ability to produce elegant code - all of that shows up in the final product, regardless of which editor I used to type it up.

AI inverts this. The tool is extremely visible in the artifact - it shapes the output’s style, structure, and polish - but the operator’s skill level becomes invisible. Everything comes out looking equally competent. You can’t tell from a pull request whether the author spent thirty minutes carefully steering the AI through edge cases or just hit accept on the first suggestion.

That’s a huge problem, really. Because before, a bad pull request was easy to spot. Oftentimes a junior engineer would give you “hints” by not following the style guides or established conventions, which eventually tips you off and leads you to discover a major bug or missed corner case.

Well, AI output always looks polished. We lost a key indicator which makes engineering spidey sense tingle. Now every line of code, every pull request is a suspect. And that’s exhausting.

Looking done versus being right

I just read Ivan Turkovic’s excellent AI Made Writing Code Easier. It Made Being an Engineer Harder (thanks for the share-out, Ben), and I couldn’t agree more with his core observation. The gap between “looking done” and “being right” is growing, and it’s growing fast.

You know what’s annoying? When your PM can prototype something in an afternoon and expects you to get that prototype “the rest of the way done” by Friday. Or the same day, if they’re feeling particularly optimistic about what “the rest of the way” means (my PMs are wonderful and thankfully don’t do this).

But either way I don’t blame them, honestly. The prototype looks great. It’s got real-ish data, it handles the happy path, and it even has a loading spinner. It looks like a product. And if I could build this in two hours with an AI tool - well, how hard could it be for a full-time engineer to finish it up?

The answer, of course, is that the last 10% of the work is 90% of the effort. Edge cases, error handling, validation, accessibility, security, performance under load, integration with existing systems, observability - none of that is visible in a prototype, and AI tools are exceptionally good at producing work that doesn’t have any of it. The prototype isn’t 90% done. It 90% looks good.

Of course there’s an education component here - understanding the difference between surface level polish and structural soundness. But there’s a deeper problem here too, and it’s hard to solve with education alone.

The empathy gap

My friend and colleague Sarah put this better than I could: we’re going to need lessons in empathy.

Here’s what she means. When a PM can spin up a working prototype in an afternoon using AI, they start to believe - even subconsciously - that they understand what engineering involves. When an engineer uses AI to generate user-facing documentation, they start to think the tech writer’s job is trivial. When a designer uses AI to write frontend code, they wonder why the team needs a dedicated frontend engineer.

And none of these people are wrong about what they experienced. The PM really did build a working prototype. The engineer really did produce passable documentation. But the conclusion that they “did the other person’s job” and the job is therefore easy - is completely wrong.

Speaking of Sarah. Sarah is a staff user experience researcher. It’s Doctor Sarah, actually. And I had the opportunity to contribute on a research paper, and I used AI to structure my contributions, and I was oh-so-proud of the work because it looked exactly like what I’ve seen in countless research papers I’ve read over the years. And Sarah scanned through my contributions, and was real proud of me. Until she sat down to read what I wrote, and had to rewrite just about everything I “contributed” from scratch.

AI gives everyone a surface-level ability to contribute across almost any domain or role. And surface-level ability is the most dangerous kind, because it comes with surface-level understanding and full-depth confidence. Modern knowledge jobs are often understood by their output. Tech writers by the documents produced, designers by the mocks, and software engineers by code. But none of those artifacts are core skills of each role. Tech writers are really good at breaking down complex concepts in ways majority of people can understand and internalize. Designers build intuition and understanding of how people behave and engage with all kinds of stuff. Software engineers solve problems. AI tools can’t do those things.

The path forward isn’t to gatekeep or to dismiss AI-generated contributions. It’s to build organizational empathy - a genuine understanding that every discipline has depth that isn’t visible from the outside, and that a tool which lets you produce artifacts in another person’s domain doesn’t mean you understand that domain.

This is, admittedly, not a new problem. Engineers have underestimated designers since the dawn of software. PMs have underestimated engineers for just as long. But AI is pouring fuel on this particular fire by making everyone feel like a competent generalist.

So what do we actually do?

I don’t want to be the person writing yet another “AI is ruining everything” essay. Frankly, there are enough of those. AI tools are genuinely useful - I use them daily, they make certain kinds of work better, and they’re here to stay. The scaffolding, the boilerplate, the “I know exactly what this should look like but I don’t want to type it out” moments - AI is great for those. Just like Vim is great for the “I need to restructure this method” moments.

A few things I think help, borrowing from Turkovic’s recommendations and adding some of my own:

Draw clear boundaries around AI output. A prototype is a prototype, not a product. AI-generated code is a first draft, not a pull request. Making this explicit - in team norms, in review processes, in how we talk about work - helps close the gap between appearance and reality.

Invest in education, not just adoption. Rolling out AI tools without teaching people how to evaluate their output is like handing someone Vim without explaining modes. They’ll produce something, sure, but they won’t understand what they produced. And unlike Vim, where the failure mode is jjjjjjkkkkkk:help! in your file, the failure mode with AI is shipping code that looks correct and isn’t.

Build empathy across disciplines. This is Sarah’s point, and I think it’s the most important one. If AI makes it easy for anyone to produce surface-level work in any domain, then we need to get much better at respecting the depth beneath the surface. That means engineers sitting with PMs to understand their constraints, PMs shadowing engineers through the painful parts of productionization, and everyone acknowledging that “I made a thing with AI” is the beginning of a conversation, not the end of one.

Protect your flow. This is the Vim lesson. The best tools are the ones that serve your intent without distorting it. If an AI tool is helping you think more clearly about the problem, great. If it’s generating so much output that your job has shifted from “solving problems” to “reviewing AI’s work” - that’s not flow. That’s a different job, and it might not be the one you signed up for.

I keep coming back to this: Vim is a good tool because it does what I mean. The gap between my intent and the output is zero. AI tools are useful, sometimes very useful, but that gap is never zero. Knowing when the gap matters and when it doesn’t - that’s a core skill for where we are today.

P.S. Did this piece need a Vim throughline? No it didn’t. But I enjoyed shoehorning it in regardless. I hear that’s going around lately.

All opinions expressed here are my own. I don’t speak for Google.

sentences.zh: 为什么。

sentences.zh: 您可以不间断地进行编辑，这可以让您的大脑有空间专注于手头的任务。

You can’t tell from a pull request whether the author spent thirty minutes carefully steering the AI through edge cases or just hit accept on the first suggestion.

sentences.en: AI inverts this.
sentences.zh: 人工智能扭转了这一点。

sentences.en: The tool is extremely visible in the artifact - it shapes the output’s style, structure, and polish - but the operator’s skill level becomes invisible.
sentences.zh: 该工具在工件中非常明显 - 它塑造了输出的风格、结构和修饰 - 但操作员的技能水平却变得不可见。

sentences.en: Everything comes out looking equally competent.
sentences.zh: 一切看起来都同样有能力。

sentences.en: You can’t tell from a pull request whether the author spent thirty minutes carefully steering the AI through edge cases or just hit accept on the first suggestion.
sentences.zh: 你无法从拉取请求中判断出作者是否花了三十分钟小心地引导人工智能通过边缘情况，或者只是在第一个建议上点击接受。

blocks.html_en: That’s a huge problem, really. Because before, a bad pull request was easy to spot. Oftentimes a junior engineer would give you “hints” by not following the style guides or established conventions, which eventually tips you off and leads you to discover a major bug or missed corner case.

sentences.en: That’s a huge problem, really.
sentences.zh: 这确实是一个大问题。

sentences.en: Because before, a bad pull request was easy to spot.
sentences.zh: 因为以前，错误的拉取请求很容易被发现。

sentences.en: Oftentimes a junior engineer would give you “hints” by not following the style guides or established conventions, which eventually tips you off and leads you to discover a major bug or missed corner case.
sentences.zh: 通常，初级工程师会通过不遵循风格指南或既定惯例来给您“提示”，这最终会给您提示并导致您发现主要错误或遗漏的极端情况。

blocks.html_en: Well, AI output always looks polished. We lost a key indicator which makes engineering spidey sense tingle. Now every line of code, every pull request is a suspect. And that’s exhausting.

sentences.en: Well, AI output always looks polished.
sentences.zh: 嗯，人工智能输出总是看起来很漂亮。

sentences.en: We lost a key indicator which makes engineering spidey sense tingle.
sentences.zh: 我们失去了一个让工程蜘蛛侠感到刺痛的关键指标。

sentences.en: Now every line of code, every pull request is a suspect.
sentences.zh: 现在每一行代码、每个拉取请求都是可疑的。

sentences.en: And that’s exhausting.
sentences.zh: 这很累人。

blocks.html_en: Looking done versus being right

sentences.en: Looking done versus being right
sentences.zh: 看起来完成与正确

blocks.html_en: I just read Ivan Turkovic’s excellent AI Made Writing Code Easier. It Made Being an Engineer Harder (thanks for the share-out, Ben), and I couldn’t agree more with his core observation. The gap between “looking done” and “being right” is growing, and it’s growing fast.

sentences.en: I just read Ivan Turkovic’s excellent AI Made Writing Code Easier.
sentences.zh: 我刚刚读了 Ivan Turkovic 的优秀著作《AI 让编写代码变得更容易》。

sentences.en: It Made Being an Engineer Harder (thanks for the share-out, Ben), and I couldn’t agree more with his core observation.
sentences.zh: 这让成为一名工程师变得更加困难（感谢 Ben 的分享），我非常同意他的核心观察。

sentences.en: The gap between “looking done” and “being right” is growing, and it’s growing fast.
sentences.zh: “看起来已经完成”和“正确”之间的差距正在拉大，而且拉得很快。

blocks.html_en: You know what’s annoying? When your PM can prototype something in an afternoon and expects you to get that prototype “the rest of the way done” by Friday. Or the same day, if they’re feeling particularly optimistic about what “the rest of the way” means (my PMs are wonderful and thankfully don’t do this).

sentences.en: You know what’s annoying?
sentences.zh: 你知道什么是烦人的吗？

sentences.en: When your PM can prototype something in an afternoon and expects you to get that prototype “the rest of the way done” by Friday.
sentences.zh: 当你的产品经理可以在一个下午制作出某个东西的原型，并希望你在周五之前“完成剩下的工作”时。

sentences.en: Or the same day, if they’re feeling particularly optimistic about what “the rest of the way” means (my PMs are wonderful and thankfully don’t do this).
sentences.zh: 或者在同一天，如果他们对“剩下的路”的含义感到特别乐观（我的产品经理很棒，值得庆幸的是他们不会这样做）。

blocks.html_en: But either way I don’t blame them, honestly. The prototype looks great. It’s got real-ish data, it handles the happy path, and it even has a loading spinner. It looks like a product. And if I could build this in two hours with an AI tool - well, how hard could it be for a full-time engineer to finish it up?

sentences.en: But either way I don’t blame them, honestly.
sentences.zh: 但无论如何，老实说，我不会责怪他们。

sentences.en: The prototype looks great.
sentences.zh: 原型看起来很棒。

sentences.en: It’s got real-ish data, it handles the happy path, and it even has a loading spinner.
sentences.zh: 它有真实的数据，它处理快乐的路径，它甚至还有一个加载旋转器。

sentences.en: It looks like a product.
sentences.zh: 它看起来像一个产品。

sentences.en: And if I could build this in two hours with an AI tool - well, how hard could it be for a full-time engineer to finish it up?
sentences.zh: 如果我可以用人工智能工具在两个小时内构建这个——那么，对于一个全职工程师来说完成它会有多难呢？

blocks.html_en: The answer, of course, is that the last 10% of the work is 90% of the effort. Edge cases, error handling, validation, accessibility, security, performance under load, integration with existing systems, observability - none of that is visible in a prototype, and AI tools are exceptionally good at producing work that doesn’t have any of it. The prototype isn’t 90% done. It 90% looks good.

sentences.en: The answer, of course, is that the last 10% of the work is 90% of the effort.
sentences.zh: 答案当然是，最后10%的工作就是90%的努力。

sentences.en: Edge cases, error handling, validation, accessibility, security, performance under load, integration with existing systems, observability - none of that is visible in a prototype, and AI tools are exceptionally good at producing work that doesn’t have any of it.
sentences.zh: 边缘情况、错误处理、验证、可访问性、安全性、负载下的性能、与现有系统的集成、可观察性——这些在原型中都是不可见的，而人工智能工具非常擅长制作没有这些的工作。

sentences.en: The prototype isn’t 90% done.
sentences.zh: 原型尚未完成 90%。

sentences.en: It 90% looks good.
sentences.zh: 90%看起来不错。

blocks.html_en: Of course there’s an education component here - understanding the difference between surface level polish and structural soundness. But there’s a deeper problem here too, and it’s hard to solve with education alone.

sentences.en: Of course there’s an education component here - understanding the difference between surface level polish and structural soundness.
sentences.zh: 当然，这里有一个教育部分——了解表面抛光和结构健全性之间的区别。

sentences.en: But there’s a deeper problem here too, and it’s hard to solve with education alone.
sentences.zh: 但这里还有一个更深层次的问题，仅靠教育很难解决。

blocks.html_en: The empathy gap

sentences.en: The empathy gap
sentences.zh: 同理心差距

blocks.html_en: My friend and colleague Sarah put this better than I could: we’re going to need lessons in empathy.

sentences.en: My friend and colleague Sarah put this better than I could: we’re going to need lessons in empathy.
sentences.zh: 我的朋友兼同事莎拉比我说得更好：我们需要学习同理心。

blocks.html_en: Here’s what she means. When a PM can spin up a working prototype in an afternoon using AI, they start to believe - even subconsciously - that they understand what engineering involves. When an engineer uses AI to generate user-facing documentation, they start to think the tech writer’s job is trivial. When a designer uses AI to write frontend code, they wonder why the team needs a dedicated frontend engineer.

sentences.en: Here’s what she means.
sentences.zh: 这就是她的意思。

sentences.en: When a PM can spin up a working prototype in an afternoon using AI, they start to believe - even subconsciously - that they understand what engineering involves.
sentences.zh: 当产品经理可以在一个下午使用人工智能启动一个工作原型时，他们开始相信（甚至是下意识的）他们了解工程涉及什么。

sentences.en: When an engineer uses AI to generate user-facing documentation, they start to think the tech writer’s job is trivial.
sentences.zh: 当工程师使用人工智能生成面向用户的文档时，他们开始认为技术作家的工作是微不足道的。

sentences.en: When a designer uses AI to write frontend code, they wonder why the team needs a dedicated frontend engineer.
sentences.zh: 当设计师使用人工智能编写前端代码时，他们想知道为什么团队需要专门的前端工程师。

blocks.html_en: And none of these people are wrong about what they experienced. The PM really did build a working prototype. The engineer really did produce passable documentation. But the conclusion that they “did the other person’s job” and the job is therefore easy - is completely wrong.

sentences.en: And none of these people are wrong about what they experienced.
sentences.zh: 这些人对于他们所经历的事情没有一个是错误的。

sentences.en: The PM really did build a working prototype.
sentences.zh: PM 确实构建了一个工作原型。

sentences.en: The engineer really did produce passable documentation.
sentences.zh: 工程师确实制作了还过得去的文档。

sentences.en: But the conclusion that they “did the other person’s job” and the job is therefore easy - is completely wrong.
sentences.zh: 但他们“做了别人的工作”并且工作因此很容易的结论是完全错误的。

blocks.html_en: Speaking of Sarah. Sarah is a staff user experience researcher. It’s Doctor Sarah, actually. And I had the opportunity to contribute on a research paper, and I used AI to structure my contributions, and I was oh-so-proud of the work because it looked exactly like what I’ve seen in countless research papers I’ve read over the years. And Sarah scanned through my contributions, and was real proud of me. Until she sat down to read what I wrote, and had to rewrite just about everything I “contributed” from scratch.

sentences.en: Speaking of Sarah.
sentences.zh: 说到莎拉.

sentences.en: Sarah is a staff user experience researcher.
sentences.zh: 莎拉是一名用户体验研究员。

sentences.en: It’s Doctor Sarah, actually.
sentences.zh: 实际上是莎拉医生。

sentences.en: And I had the opportunity to contribute on a research paper, and I used AI to structure my contributions, and I was oh-so-proud of the work because it looked exactly like what I’ve seen in countless research papers I’ve read over the years.
sentences.zh: 我有机会为一篇研究论文做出贡献，我使用人工智能来构建我的贡献，我对这项工作感到非常自豪，因为它看起来与我多年来读过的无数研究论文中看到的一模一样。

sentences.en: And Sarah scanned through my contributions, and was real proud of me.
sentences.zh: 莎拉浏览了我的贡献，并为我感到骄傲。

sentences.en: Until she sat down to read what I wrote, and had to rewrite just about everything I “contributed” from scratch.
sentences.zh: 直到她坐下来阅读我写的内容，并且不得不从头开始重写我“贡献”的所有内容。

blocks.html_en: AI gives everyone a surface-level ability to contribute across almost any domain or role. And surface-level ability is the most dangerous kind, because it comes with surface-level understanding and full-depth confidence. Modern knowledge jobs are often understood by their output. Tech writers by the documents produced, designers by the mocks, and software engineers by code. But none of those artifacts are core skills of each role. Tech writers are really good at breaking down complex concepts in ways majority of people can understand and internalize. Designers build intuition and understanding of how people behave and engage with all kinds of stuff. Software engineers solve problems. AI tools can’t do those things.

sentences.en: AI gives everyone a surface-level ability to contribute across almost any domain or role.
sentences.zh: 人工智能为每个人提供了在几乎任何领域或角色上做出贡献的表面能力。

sentences.en: And surface-level ability is the most dangerous kind, because it comes with surface-level understanding and full-depth confidence.
sentences.zh: 而表面的能力是最危险的，因为它带有表面的理解和全面的深度自信。

sentences.en: Modern knowledge jobs are often understood by their output.
sentences.zh: 现代知识工作通常通过其产出来理解。

sentences.en: Tech writers by the documents produced, designers by the mocks, and software engineers by code.
sentences.zh: 技术作家通过生成的文档，设计师通过模拟，软件工程师通过代码。

sentences.en: But none of those artifacts are core skills of each role.
sentences.zh: 但这些工件都不是每个角色的核心技能。

sentences.en: Tech writers are really good at breaking down complex concepts in ways majority of people can understand and internalize.
sentences.zh: 科技作家非常擅长以大多数人可以理解和内化的方式分解复杂的概念。

sentences.en: Designers build intuition and understanding of how people behave and engage with all kinds of stuff.
sentences.zh: 设计师建立对人们如何行为以及如何与各种事物互动的直觉和理解。

sentences.en: Software engineers solve problems.
sentences.zh: 软件工程师解决问题。

sentences.en: AI tools can’t do those things.
sentences.zh: 人工智能工具无法做到这些事情。

blocks.html_en: The path forward isn’t to gatekeep or to dismiss AI-generated contributions. It’s to build organizational empathy - a genuine understanding that every discipline has depth that isn’t visible from the outside, and that a tool which lets you produce artifacts in another person’s domain doesn’t mean you understand that domain.

sentences.en: The path forward isn’t to gatekeep or to dismiss AI-generated contributions.
sentences.zh: 前进的道路不是把关或拒绝人工智能产生的贡献。

sentences.en: It’s to build organizational empathy - a genuine understanding that every discipline has depth that isn’t visible from the outside, and that a tool which lets you produce artifacts in another person’s domain doesn’t mean you understand that domain.
sentences.zh: 这是为了建立组织同理心 - 真正理解每个学科都有从外部看不到的深度，并且让您在另一个人的领域中生成工件的工具并不意味着您了解该领域。

blocks.html_en: This is, admittedly, not a new problem. Engineers have underestimated designers since the dawn of software. PMs have underestimated engineers for just as long. But AI is pouring fuel on this particular fire by making everyone feel like a competent generalist.

sentences.en: This is, admittedly, not a new problem.
sentences.zh: 诚然，这并不是一个新问题。

sentences.en: Engineers have underestimated designers since the dawn of software.
sentences.zh: 自软件出现以来，工程师就低估了设计师。

sentences.en: PMs have underestimated engineers for just as long.
sentences.zh: 产品经理长期以来一直低估工程师。

sentences.en: But AI is pouring fuel on this particular fire by making everyone feel like a competent generalist.
sentences.zh: 但人工智能正在火上浇油，让每个人都觉得自己是一位有能力的多面手。

blocks.html_en: So what do we actually do?

sentences.en: So what do we actually do?
sentences.zh: 那么我们实际上该怎么做呢？

blocks.html_en: I don’t want to be the person writing yet another “AI is ruining everything” essay. Frankly, there are enough of those. AI tools are genuinely useful - I use them daily, they make certain kinds of work better, and they’re here to stay. The scaffolding, the boilerplate, the “I know exactly what this should look like but I don’t want to type it out” moments - AI is great for those. Just like Vim is great for the “I need to restructure this method” moments.

sentences.en: I don’t want to be the person writing yet another “AI is ruining everything” essay.
sentences.zh: 我不想成为又一篇“人工智能正在毁掉一切”文章的人。

sentences.en: Frankly, there are enough of those.
sentences.zh: 坦率地说，这些已经足够了。

sentences.en: AI tools are genuinely useful - I use them daily, they make certain kinds of work better, and they’re here to stay.
sentences.zh: 人工智能工具确实很有用——我每天都使用它们，它们使某些类型的工作变得更好，并且它们会一直存在。

sentences.en: The scaffolding, the boilerplate, the “I know exactly what this should look like but I don’t want to type it out” moments - AI is great for those.
sentences.zh: 脚手架、样板、“我确切地知道它应该是什么样子，但我不想把它打印出来”的时刻——人工智能非常适合这些。

sentences.en: Just like Vim is great for the “I need to restructure this method” moments.
sentences.zh: 就像 Vim 非常适合“我需要重组这个方法”的时刻。

blocks.html_en: A few things I think help, borrowing from Turkovic’s recommendations and adding some of my own:

sentences.en: A few things I think help, borrowing from Turkovic’s recommendations and adding some of my own:
sentences.zh: 我认为有一些有用的东西，借鉴了图尔科维奇的建议并添加了我自己的一些建议：

blocks.html_en: Draw clear boundaries around AI output. A prototype is a prototype, not a product. AI-generated code is a first draft, not a pull request. Making this explicit - in team norms, in review processes, in how we talk about work - helps close the gap between appearance and reality.

sentences.en: Draw clear boundaries around AI output.
sentences.zh: 围绕人工智能输出划出清晰的界限。

sentences.en: A prototype is a prototype, not a product.
sentences.zh: 原型只是原型，而不是产品。

sentences.en: AI-generated code is a first draft, not a pull request.
sentences.zh: 人工智能生成的代码是初稿，而不是拉取请求。

sentences.en: Making this explicit - in team norms, in review processes, in how we talk about work - helps close the gap between appearance and reality.
sentences.zh: 在团队规范、审查流程以及我们如何谈论工作中明确这一点有助于缩小表象与现实之间的差距。

blocks.html_en: Invest in education, not just adoption. Rolling out AI tools without teaching people how to evaluate their output is like handing someone Vim without explaining modes. They’ll produce something, sure, but they won’t understand what they produced. And unlike Vim, where the failure mode is jjjjjjkkkkkk:help! in your file, the failure mode with AI is shipping code that looks correct and isn’t.

sentences.en: Invest in education, not just adoption.
sentences.zh: 投资于教育，而不仅仅是收养。

sentences.en: Rolling out AI tools without teaching people how to evaluate their output is like handing someone Vim without explaining modes.
sentences.zh: 推出人工智能工具而不教人们如何评估其输出就像递给某人 Vim 而不解释模式一样。

sentences.en: They’ll produce something, sure, but they won’t understand what they produced.
sentences.zh: 当然，他们会生产一些东西，但他们不会理解他们生产了什么。

sentences.en: And unlike Vim, where the failure mode is jjjjjjkkkkkk:help!
sentences.zh: 与 Vim 不同的是，Vim 的故障模式是 jjjjjjkkkkkk:help!

sentences.en: in your file, the failure mode with AI is shipping code that looks correct and isn’t.
sentences.zh: 在你的文件中，人工智能的失败模式是交付看起来正确但实际上不正确的代码。

blocks.html_en: Build empathy across disciplines. This is Sarah’s point, and I think it’s the most important one. If AI makes it easy for anyone to produce surface-level work in any domain, then we need to get much better at respecting the depth beneath the surface. That means engineers sitting with PMs to understand their constraints, PMs shadowing engineers through the painful parts of productionization, and everyone acknowledging that “I made a thing with AI” is the beginning of a conversation, not the end of one.

sentences.en: Build empathy across disciplines.
sentences.zh: 建立跨学科的同理心。

sentences.en: This is Sarah’s point, and I think it’s the most important one.
sentences.zh: 这是莎拉的观点，我认为这是最重要的一点。

sentences.en: If AI makes it easy for anyone to produce surface-level work in any domain, then we need to get much better at respecting the depth beneath the surface.
sentences.zh: 如果人工智能让任何人都能轻松地在任何领域进行表面工作，那么我们就需要更好地尊重表面之下的深度。

sentences.en: That means engineers sitting with PMs to understand their constraints, PMs shadowing engineers through the painful parts of productionization, and everyone acknowledging that “I made a thing with AI” is the beginning of a conversation, not the end of one.
sentences.zh: 这意味着工程师与产品经理坐在一起了解他们的限制，产品经理跟踪工程师经历生产化的痛苦部分，并且每个人都承认“我用人工智能做了一件事情”是对话的开始，而不是结束。

blocks.html_en: Protect your flow. This is the Vim lesson. The best tools are the ones that serve your intent without distorting it. If an AI tool is helping you think more clearly about the problem, great. If it’s generating so much output that your job has shifted from “solving problems” to “reviewing AI’s work” - that’s not flow. That’s a different job, and it might not be the one you signed up for.

sentences.en: Protect your flow.
sentences.zh: 保护您的流量。

sentences.en: This is the Vim lesson.
sentences.zh: 这是 Vim 课程。

sentences.en: The best tools are the ones that serve your intent without distorting it.
sentences.zh: 最好的工具是那些能够满足您的意图而不扭曲它的工具。

sentences.en: If an AI tool is helping you think more clearly about the problem, great.
sentences.zh: 如果人工智能工具可以帮助您更清楚地思考问题，那就太好了。

sentences.en: If it’s generating so much output that your job has shifted from “solving problems” to “reviewing AI’s work” - that’s not flow.
sentences.zh: 如果它产生如此多的输出，以至于你的工作已经从“解决问题”转变为“审查人工智能的工作”——那就不是心流。

sentences.en: That’s a different job, and it might not be the one you signed up for.
sentences.zh: 那是另一份工作，可能不是您报名的工作。

blocks.html_en: I keep coming back to this: Vim is a good tool because it does what I mean. The gap between my intent and the output is zero. AI tools are useful, sometimes very useful, but that gap is never zero. Knowing when the gap matters and when it doesn’t - that’s a core skill for where we are today.

sentences.en: I keep coming back to this: Vim is a good tool because it does what I mean.
sentences.zh: 我不断地回到这一点：Vim 是一个很好的工具，因为它实现了我的意思。

sentences.en: The gap between my intent and the output is zero.
sentences.zh: 我的意图和输出之间的差距为零。

sentences.en: AI tools are useful, sometimes very useful, but that gap is never zero.
sentences.zh: 人工智能工具很有用，有时非常有用，但这种差距永远不会为零。

sentences.en: Knowing when the gap matters and when it doesn’t - that’s a core skill for where we are today.
sentences.zh: 知道差距何时重要、何时不重要——这是我们今天所处的核心技能。

blocks.html_en: P.S. Did this piece need a Vim throughline? No it didn’t. But I enjoyed shoehorning it in regardless. I hear that’s going around lately.

sentences.en: P.S.
sentences.zh: 附：

sentences.en: Did this piece need a Vim throughline?
sentences.zh: 这件作品需要 Vim 贯穿线吗？

sentences.en: No it didn’t.
sentences.zh: 不，没有。

sentences.en: But I enjoyed shoehorning it in regardless.
sentences.zh: 但我还是喜欢硬塞进去。

sentences.en: I hear that’s going around lately.
sentences.zh: 我听说最近有这种事。

blocks.html_en: All opinions expressed here are my own. I don’t speak for Google.

sentences.en: All opinions expressed here are my own.
sentences.zh: 这里表达的所有意见都是我自己的。

sentences.en: I don’t speak for Google.
sentences.zh: 我不代表谷歌发言。

content_html_zh:
最近，我在工作中更多地使用人工智能——它正在成为整个行业的明确期望。 编写更多代码、提供更多功能、更快交付。 你知道这让我想到什么吗？ 为什么。

我会自己解释的，别担心。

我喜欢维姆。 足够写一本关于编辑器的书，也足够使用 Vim 来写这篇文章。 我确信您遇到过对 Vim 或 Emacs 设置深信不疑的同事，或者您自己也可能是其中之一。

大多数人对 Vim 的误解是：它与速度无关。 它不一定会让你更快（尽管可以），但它的作用是让你保持流畅。 它使文本编辑变得更加容易——不用费力地寻找鼠标或按住箭头键三秒半，这真是太好了。 你可以直接删除一句话。 或者替换括号内的文本，或者将括号替换为引号。 您可以不间断地进行编辑，这可以让您的大脑有空间专注于手头的任务。

人工智能工具表面上看起来是这样的。 他们承诺的和 Vim 提供的一样：更少的摩擦，更多的流畅，你的大脑可以腾出时间去思考困难的事情。 有时他们确实兑现了这一承诺！ 在我的课程中，人工智能助手帮助我跳过繁琐的脚手架，直接跳入有趣的架构问题。 这里有很多好东西。

嗯，我认为 AI 和 Vim 之间的差异解释了工程师现在感受到的许多不适。

深度问题

当我使用 Vim 时，输出是我的。 每一次击键、每一个动作、每一次编辑——都是我意图的直接体现。 Vim 是一个透明的工具：它完全按照我的指示去做，仅此而已。 技能下限和上限很高，但关系很诚实。 我学习了一个新动作，我了解它的作用，并且我可以永远预测它的行为。 没有幻觉。 ci) 将始终更改括号内的文本。 有时它不会更改整个段落，因为它误解了上下文。

人工智能工具与其操作者有着不同的关系。 输出看起来像你的，读起来像你的，而且看起来肯定比你在第一次通过时生成的更精致。 但这并不是你意图的直接翻译。 有时这是一个很好的近似。 有时，它的错误方式很微妙，除非隐藏的错误进入生产环境，否则您无法发现。

这就是我所说的深度问题。 当我使用 Vim 时，没有人能从我的代码中看出我是用 Vim、VS Code 还是记事本编写的。 该工具在工件中是不可见的。 这很好，甚至很棒 - 因为输出的质量仍然完全取决于我。 我对问题的理解、我对代码库的经验、我对边缘情况的判断、我生成优雅代码的能力——所有这些都会显示在最终产品中，无论我使用哪个编辑器来输入它。

人工智能扭转了这一点。 该工具在工件中非常明显 - 它塑造了输出的风格、结构和修饰 - 但操作员的技能水平却变得不可见。 一切看起来都同样有能力。 你无法从拉取请求中判断出作者是否花了三十分钟小心地引导人工智能通过边缘情况，或者只是在第一个建议上点击接受。

这确实是一个大问题。 因为以前，错误的拉取请求很容易被发现。 通常，初级工程师会通过不遵循风格指南或既定惯例来给您“提示”，这最终会给您提示并导致您发现主要错误或遗漏的极端情况。

嗯，人工智能输出总是看起来很漂亮。 我们失去了一个让工程蜘蛛侠感到刺痛的关键指标。 现在每一行代码、每个拉取请求都是可疑的。 这很累人。

看起来完成与正确

我刚刚读了 Ivan Turkovic 的优秀著作《AI 让编写代码变得更容易》。 这让成为一名工程师变得更加困难（感谢 Ben 的分享），我非常同意他的核心观察。 “看起来已经完成”和“正确”之间的差距正在拉大，而且拉得很快。

你知道什么是烦人的吗？ 当你的产品经理可以在一个下午制作出某个东西的原型，并希望你在周五之前“完成剩下的工作”时。 或者在同一天，如果他们对“剩下的路”的含义感到特别乐观（我的产品经理很棒，值得庆幸的是他们不会这样做）。

但无论如何，老实说，我不会责怪他们。 原型看起来很棒。 它有真实的数据，它处理快乐的路径，它甚至还有一个加载旋转器。 它看起来像一个产品。 如果我可以用人工智能工具在两个小时内构建这个——那么，对于一个全职工程师来说完成它会有多难呢？

答案当然是，最后10%的工作就是90%的努力。 边缘情况、错误处理、验证、可访问性、安全性、负载下的性能、与现有系统的集成、可观察性——这些在原型中都是不可见的，而人工智能工具非常擅长制作没有这些的工作。 原型尚未完成 90%。 90%看起来不错。

当然，这里有一个教育部分——了解表面抛光和结构健全性之间的区别。 但这里还有一个更深层次的问题，仅靠教育很难解决。

同理心差距

我的朋友兼同事莎拉比我说得更好：我们需要学习同理心。

这就是她的意思。 当产品经理可以在一个下午使用人工智能启动一个工作原型时，他们开始相信（甚至是下意识的）他们了解工程涉及什么。 当工程师使用人工智能生成面向用户的文档时，他们开始认为技术作家的工作是微不足道的。 当设计师使用人工智能编写前端代码时，他们想知道为什么团队需要专门的前端工程师。

这些人对于他们所经历的事情没有一个是错误的。 PM 确实构建了一个工作原型。 工程师确实制作了还过得去的文档。 但他们“做了别人的工作”并且工作因此很容易的结论是完全错误的。

说到莎拉. 莎拉是一名用户体验研究员。 实际上是莎拉医生。 我有机会为一篇研究论文做出贡献，我使用人工智能来构建我的贡献，我对这项工作感到非常自豪，因为它看起来与我多年来读过的无数研究论文中看到的一模一样。 莎拉浏览了我的贡献，并为我感到骄傲。 直到她坐下来阅读我写的内容，并且不得不从头开始重写我“贡献”的所有内容。

人工智能为每个人提供了在几乎任何领域或角色上做出贡献的表面能力。 而表面的能力是最危险的，因为它带有表面的理解和全面的深度自信。 现代知识工作通常通过其产出来理解。 技术作家通过生成的文档，设计师通过模拟，软件工程师通过代码。 但这些工件都不是每个角色的核心技能。 科技作家非常擅长以大多数人可以理解和内化的方式分解复杂的概念。 设计师建立对人们如何行为以及如何与各种事物互动的直觉和理解。 软件工程师解决问题。 人工智能工具无法做到这些事情。

前进的道路不是把关或拒绝人工智能产生的贡献。 这是为了建立组织同理心 - 真正理解每个学科都有从外部看不到的深度，并且让您在另一个人的领域中生成工件的工具并不意味着您了解该领域。

诚然，这并不是一个新问题。 自软件出现以来，工程师就低估了设计师。 产品经理长期以来一直低估工程师。 但人工智能正在火上浇油，让每个人都觉得自己是一位有能力的多面手。

那么我们实际上该怎么做呢？

我不想成为又一篇“人工智能正在毁掉一切”文章的人。 坦率地说，这些已经足够了。 人工智能工具确实很有用——我每天都使用它们，它们使某些类型的工作变得更好，并且它们会一直存在。 脚手架、样板、“我确切地知道它应该是什么样子，但我不想把它打印出来”的时刻——人工智能非常适合这些。 就像 Vim 非常适合“我需要重组这个方法”的时刻。

我认为有一些有用的东西，借鉴了图尔科维奇的建议并添加了我自己的一些建议：

围绕人工智能输出划出清晰的界限。 原型只是原型，而不是产品。 人工智能生成的代码是初稿，而不是拉取请求。 在团队规范、审查流程以及我们如何谈论工作中明确这一点有助于缩小表象与现实之间的差距。

投资于教育，而不仅仅是收养。 推出人工智能工具而不教人们如何评估其输出就像递给某人 Vim 而不解释模式一样。 当然，他们会生产一些东西，但他们不会理解他们生产了什么。 与 Vim 不同的是，Vim 的故障模式是 jjjjjjkkkkkk:help! 在你的文件中，人工智能的失败模式是交付看起来正确但实际上不正确的代码。

建立跨学科的同理心。 这是莎拉的观点，我认为这是最重要的一点。 如果人工智能让任何人都能轻松地在任何领域进行表面工作，那么我们就需要更好地尊重表面之下的深度。 这意味着工程师与产品经理坐在一起了解他们的限制，产品经理跟踪工程师经历生产化的痛苦部分，并且每个人都承认“我用人工智能做了一件事情”是对话的开始，而不是结束。

保护您的流量。 这是 Vim 课程。 最好的工具是那些能够满足您的意图而不扭曲它的工具。 如果人工智能工具可以帮助您更清楚地思考问题，那就太好了。 如果它产生如此多的输出，以至于你的工作已经从“解决问题”转变为“审查人工智能的工作”——那就不是心流。 那是另一份工作，可能不是您报名的工作。

我不断地回到这一点：Vim 是一个很好的工具，因为它实现了我的意思。 我的意图和输出之间的差距为零。 人工智能工具很有用，有时非常有用，但这种差距永远不会为零。 知道差距何时重要、何时不重要——这是我们今天所处的核心技能。

附： 这件作品需要 Vim 贯穿线吗？ 不，没有。 但我还是喜欢硬塞进去。 我听说最近有这种事。

这里表达的所有意见都是我自己的。 我不代表谷歌发言。
